comments.text: My colleague Johnny Yu has run this query on GPT 5.2 Pro that reduces this to a graph problem, then applies an existing result from Alon-Pham to produce a tighter upper bound: https://chatgpt.com/share/696e0e87-b834-8012-b0b1-cd9829fe3d21 They apply the Alon-Pham result (Theorem 4) by constructing a graph $G_B$ of vertices $\{n, ... 2n\}$, then drawing an edge if $v_1 + v_2 \in B$. Then a set $C$ is "admissible" if $C$ is an independent set in $G$, where the best possible size of $C$ is $\alpha(G_B)$. So they try to minimize $\min_B(|B| + \alpha(G_B))$. This graph can be transformed into the same setting that Alon-Pham study, where they show that w.h.p. a full random Cayley sum graph $S$ has independence number at most $O(p^{2/3})$, where $|S|$ is typically $O(np)$. Solving for the optimal $p$ gives $|B|$ + $\alpha(G_B) << n^{3/5}\log(n)^{O(1)}$. It doesn't solve whether $f(n) \le n^{1/2 + o(1)}$, but maybe worth updating the body of the problem to include this result. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

ChatGPT Pro is broadly okay with this argument, though it points out some typos and minor issues with the exposition. I guess the next steps would be a more human-generated version of the proof, and/or a conditional formalization in Lean using Alon-Pham's Theorem 4 as an axiom? The latter appears moderately challenging with current autoformalization tools, but not impossible.
Sorry about that, the $O(p^{2/3})$ was a typo when I was rewriting the proof in my own words. Agreed on the next steps.

I've generated this Lean file using Aristotle, using the Alon-Pham result as an assumption. Posting here for verification: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1xNftrwtx5_cKpUa7sIk3-b4fQ-6IvmBL/view?usp=drivesdk

Since the 'MainAssumption' here is from ChatGPT's interpretation of the Alon-Pham result, I've done an alternative formalization which starts from formalizing the statement from the Alon-Pham paper first, without the ChatGPT text, to be safe. I describe my process and results here. Type-check it online!

Thanks! In fact this is the same idea as given by Zach Hunter below, but taking advantage of the new independence number bounds by Alon and Pham (which came out after Zach posted his comment). Zach used the previous bound of $\alpha(G) \lesssim p^{-2}$, which balanced against $\ll pn$ yields $p\approx n^{-1/3}$ and hence $n^{2/3+o(1)}$. The better Alon-Pham bound has $\lesssim p^{-3/2}$, whence $p\approx n^{-2/5}$ and hence $n^{3/5+o(1)}$. The conjectured bound of $\lesssim n^{1/2+o(1)}$ would follow from the conjectured truth about independence number of Cayley graphs (see conjecture 2 of Alon and Pham). So the reduction to the graph theory problem here is not new, but thanks for pointing out the improved bound from the Alon-Pham paper. I agree with Tao that it would be good (and presumably straightforward) to formalise this reduction, ideally with a result like 'the independence of the random Cayley graph $G(p)$ is $\tilde{O}(p^{-c})$ as an axiom, and then the formalisation would prove that this assumption implies an exponent of $\frac{c}{c+1}$ for this problem.

The argument looks plausible enough to me that I am classifying it as a Section 1 partial result on the wiki (the only thing that was really in doubt was the arithmetic, but this seems to have been confirmed). Would still be good to have a formal and/or human-generated version of the proof, of course.

This feels more like a Section 3 to me, since it is the observation of Zach Hunter (recorded in a comment below rather than published literature), coupled with an out of the box recent literature result on the independence number of random Cayley graphs.

I think you're right. The prompt didn't mention Zach's result, but it was recorded on the site and is relevant, so it meets the criteria for Section 3. (I've tried to add disclaimers that not all autonomous AI solutions necessarily go into Section 1, because sometimes we need to record relevant additional literature or inputs, even if they were not explicitly given to the AI tool.)

Indeed, given how much the site is now regularly scraped for training data, I suspect that with any Erdős-style question an LLM's internal process (at least judging from the 'in process' GPT Pro dialogue I've observed) first goes 1) check the Erdős problems site to see if this problem or something similar appears there and 2) use any suggested approaches or ideas contained there as further prompts for literature search/generating a solution.

From CoT, the first prompt doesn't seem to generate a web search. But the second prompt mentions "open Erdos problem" and CoT mentions opening this 788 thread. Hence it is most likely built on Zach's comment.

A bound of $O((n \log n)^{2/3})$ was actually shown previously by Baltz-Schoen-Srivastav in 1999 ("Probabilistic construction of small strongly sum-free sets via large Sidon sets"; Colloquium Mathematicum. Vol. 86. No. 2. Polska Akademia Nauk. Instytut Matematyczny PAN, 2000). (The reference was located by GPT-5.) (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I think it is likely that if one takes $B$ to be a random set of density $(\log n)^{100}/\sqrt{n}$, then whp $(C+C)\cap B$ is non-empty for all $C$ of size $\sqrt{n}(\log n)^{100}$. indeed, morally, we care about the independence number of the Cayley sum graph generated by $B$ (taking everything modulo $p$ for $p \sim n$). I believe this Cayley graph behaves essentially like a random binomial graph $G$ with the same number of vertices and edges (see: https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.09361 for such a result at a density of $1/\text{polylog}(n)$). one can get an improved upper bound of $f(n) \le n^{2/3+o(1)}$, by considering random $B$ of density $\approx n^{-1/3}$. the point is that we get a $n^{2/3+o(1)}$-regular Cayley graph with eigenvalues of size at most $n^{1/3+o(1)}$ (see: Proposition 6' from https://web.math.princeton.edu/~nalon/PDFS/exp1.pdf). therefore by expander mixing lemma, any set of size $n^{2/3+\Omega(1)}$ should contain edges. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The lower bound of $n^{1/2}$ is clear. Take any $B=\{b_1<\ldots<b_t\}\subset(2n,4n)\cap\mathbb{N}$ such that $|B|=g(n)$. Then, letting $2n=b_0$ and $4n=b_{t+1}$, we have $\max_{0\leq i\leq t}\{b_{i+1}-b_i\}\geq \frac{2n}{g(n)+1}$. Denote some $i\in\{0,\ldots,t\}$ that satisfies this as $i'$ and choose $C$ to be the interval $(\frac{b_{i'}}{2},\frac{b_{{i'}+1}}{2})\cap\mathbb{N}$. So $C\subset(n,2n)$ and $|B|+|C|\geq g(n)+\frac{n}{g(n)+1}-1\geq 2\sqrt{n}-2$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
